citations: Macroeconomic labor supply elasticity literature emphasizing intertemporal substitution
Calibration practices in lifecycle and dynamic general equilibrium models
Empirical studies distinguishing intensive from extensive margin responses
runs.reasoningSummary: The Frisch elasticity of labor supply represents intertemporal substitution elasticity, holding wealth effects constant. Extensive macro and labor literature suggests intensive-margin elasticities are typically low to moderate for prime-age workers. Standard calibrations in lifecycle models use values in the 0.2–0.4 range. The distribution reflects uncertainty about true responsiveness: some structural estimates suggest elasticity near 0.1–0.2, while others (e.g., quasi-experimental designs isolating substitution) yield higher estimates around 0.3–0.5. I center near 0.25 with a fairly wide distribution (0.05 to 0.60) to reflect genuine heterogeneity across studies and populations.

- Definition: Percent change in annual hours worked from a 1 percent change in the net-of-tax wage, holding the marginal utility of wealth constant and excluding participation responses.
- Target interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours Frisch elasticity used in lifecycle or macro calibration
- Population/context: Prime-age workers in the United States who are already working
- Units: elasticity

2. Give your subjective quantiles p05, p25, p50, p75, and p95 for this quantity.
3. Set `point_estimate` equal to `p50`.
4. Make the quantiles weakly increasing and numerically coherent.
5. In `citations`, list up to 3 source anchors from memory that influenced your belief. These are recall anchors only. If none come to mind confidently, return `[]`.
6. Keep `reasoning_summary` brief and substantive.

runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours Frisch elasticity for prime-age U.S. workers: percent change in annual hours worked from a 1% change in net-of-tax wage, holding marginal utility of wealth constant and excluding participation responses.
citations: Frisch (1932) on labor supply response to wage changes
Pistaferri and Peri work on wage elasticities and hours
Chetty et al. (2013) review of labor supply elasticities and bounds
runs.reasoningSummary: The Frisch elasticity is typically estimated lower than unconditional labor supply elasticities because it controls for wealth effects. Macro calibrations commonly use values in the 0.3-0.5 range for the intensive margin. This reflects modest but meaningful hours responses to temporary wage changes in the lifecycle framework, with considerable uncertainty across identification strategies and populations. Prime-age workers show lower elasticities than secondary earners.

runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours Frisch elasticity for prime-age U.S. workers already in the labor force: percent change in annual hours worked from a 1 percent change in net-of-tax wage, holding marginal utility of wealth constant and excluding participation responses.
citations: Keane and Rogerson (2012) labor supply surveys
Chetty et al. (2013) on Frisch vs. compensated elasticities
Macro calibration studies using 0.2–0.3 range
runs.reasoningSummary: The Frisch elasticity is structurally smaller than compensated elasticity because it holds wealth constant rather than utility. Prime-age worker intensive-margin estimates cluster in the 0.2–0.4 range in macro calibrations and reduced-form studies. The p50 of 0.25 reflects a moderate supply response; the interquartile range (0.15–0.40) captures the genuine uncertainty in the literature, which has varied across identification strategies and data periods. The right tail extends to 0.60 to account for higher estimates in some quasi-experimental settings, though these are less commonly used for intensive-margin Frisch calibration.

runs.reasoningSummary: The Frisch elasticity (intensive margin only, excluding participation) for prime-age US workers is substantially smaller than the compensated or Marshallian elasticity. runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours Frisch elasticity for prime-age working adults in the United States
citations: Chetty et al. (2011, AER) on empirical labor supply elasticities
Keane and Rogerson (2012) macro labor supply survey
Bargain and Orsini (2006) on Frisch vs Hicksian elasticities
runs.reasoningSummary: Frisch elasticity (holding marginal utility of wealth constant) is typically smaller than Hicksian elasticity for working-age workers. Empirical estimates for intensive-margin hours responses range widely, but central estimates tend to cluster around 0.2–0.3 for prime-age workers in the US. I place the median at 0.25 with substantial uncertainty, reflecting heterogeneity in study design, estimation method, and population subgroups. The distribution is moderately right-skewed because some structural models and quasi-experimental evidence support higher elasticities, while others find lower values.

runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours Frisch elasticity for prime-age workers in the US who are already employed: percent change in hours worked from a 1 percent change in net-of-tax wage, holding marginal utility of wealth constant.
citations: Frisch (1932) seminal work on labor supply elasticity
Lifecycle macro models typically calibrate intensive-margin Frisch elasticities between 0.2 and 0.5
Microeconometric labor supply studies suggest relatively modest intensive-margin responses
runs.reasoningSummary: The Frisch elasticity isolates substitution effects absent intertemporal wealth effects, relevant for steady-state calibration. Empirical evidence from micro labor supply studies and macro lifecycle models suggests the intensive margin (hours conditional on working) is relatively inelastic compared to extensive margin effects. My central estimate reflects common calibration values in macroeconomic models, which typically range 0.2–0.5. The distribution is right-skewed to reflect uncertainty and the possibility of somewhat larger elasticities, but most of the mass is concentrated on modest values reflecting empirical consensus that prime-age workers exhibit relatively low wage elasticity of hours on the intensive margin.
runs.rawResponse: {"interpretation": "Intensive-margin annual-hours Frisch elasticity for prime-age workers in the US who are already employed: percent change in hours worked from a 1 percent change in net-of-tax wage, holding marginal utility of wealth constant.", "point_estimate": 0.25, "quantiles": {"p05": 0.05, "p25": 0.15, "p50": 0.25, "p75": 0.4, "p95": 0.6}, "citations": ["Frisch (1932) seminal work on labor supply elasticity", "Lifecycle macro models typically calibrate intensive-margin Frisch elasticities between 0.2 and 0.5", "Microeconometric labor supply studies suggest relatively modest intensive-margin responses"], "reasoning_summary": "The Frisch elasticity isolates substitution effects absent intertemporal wealth effects, relevant for steady-state calibration. Empirical evidence from micro labor supply studies and macro lifecycle models suggests the intensive margin (hours conditional on working) is relatively inelastic compared to extensive margin effects. My central estimate reflects common calibration values in macroeconomic models, which typically range 0.2\u20130.5. The distribution is right-skewed to reflect uncertainty and the possibility of somewhat larger elasticities, but most of the mass is concentrated on modest values reflecting empirical consensus that prime-age workers exhibit relatively low wage elasticity of hours on the intensive margin."}
